title: We are free to change the world: Hannah Arendt's lessons in love and disobedience [Book Review]
author: Fanga

opening_move.description: The review opens by presenting Stonebridge's book as a timely and profound exploration of Arendt's enduring relevance for resisting authoritarianism, mendacity, and dehumanisation in the twenty-first century — framing Arendt not as a historical figure but as a 'vibrant and necessary companion'.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Arendt's philosophy of freedom, love, and disobedience offers hard-won strategies for survival and resistance in contemporary dark times

theoretical_positioning.description: Appreciative and expository review of Stonebridge's accessible intellectual biography-cum-political argument. The reviewer endorses Stonebridge's approach of weaving biography with philosophical analysis to make Arendt practically relevant.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Stonebridge; intellectual biography; engagement with Arendt's major concepts
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review (4 pages). Primarily expository and appreciative. The review presents Stonebridge's normative argument (Arendt as guide for contemporary resistance) without independent critique.

case_type.case_name: Stonebridge, We Are Free to Change the World: Hannah Arendt's Lessons in Love and Disobedience (2024)

confidence_notes: Book review (4 pages). Primarily expository. The normative stake is medium rather than high because the reviewer does not independently advance the argument — Stonebridge's argument is reported, not developed.
